instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: The last element of the p-block in 6^(th) period is represented by the outermost electronic configuration:
questions.question_html: The last element of the p-block in 6th period is represented by the outermost electronic configuration:
questions.explanation: The last element of p-block in 6^(th)_( )period is _(86)Rn with outermost configuration 4f^(14) 5d^(10) 6s^(2) 6p^(2).

questions.question: Usings s,p,d, and f notation, choose the orbital with n=4, l=3 quantum numbers.
questions.question_html: Usings s,p,d, and f notation, choose the orbital with n=4, l=3 quantum numbers.
questions.explanation: Here n= principal quantum number, l= azimuthal quantum number. For n = 4 and l = 3 The orbital is 4f (can have a maximum of 14 electrons).

questions.question: Among the following elements, which has the least electron affinity?
questions.question_html: Among the following elements, which has the least electron affinity?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen has electronic configuration of 1s^(2) 2s^(2) 2p^(3). It has half-filled p orbitals in this outermost shell which is a stable configuration. It is difficult to add electron in such orbitals and lesser energy is released on the addition of electrons hence the electron affinity value will decrease.

questions.question: When a gas phase atom in its ground state gains an electron, this is called ______.
questions.question_html: When a gas phase atom in its ground state gains an electron, this is called ______.
questions.explanation: When a gas-phase atom in its ground state gains an electron, this is called Electron gain enthalpy.

questions.question: Consider the elements: Cs, Ne, I and F. Identify the element that exhibits both positive and negative oxidation states.
questions.question_html: Consider the elements: Cs, Ne, I and F. Identify the element that exhibits both positive and negative oxidation states.
questions.explanation: Iodine has 7 electrons in its valency shell and can, therefore, exhibit an oxidation state of –1, However since Iodine has empty d orbitals it can also exhibit different oxidation state like +1,+3,+5, +7.

questions.question: BaCl_(2).2H_(2)O is an example of:
questions.question_html: BaCl2.2H2O is an example of:
questions.explanation:
Water present in the interstitial sites in a crystal lattice is known as interstitial water. They are not held by hydrogen bonding with the other elements. If they are bonded they are called H-bonded hydrates.

Barium chloride is a white crystalline powder. It contains two molecules of water. It is one of the most important water-soluble salts of Barium.

questions.question: The large number of organic compounds is due to
questions.question_html: The large number of organic compounds is due to

options.text: both catenation and the tetravalency of carbon
options.html: both catenation and the tetravalency of carbon

correct_options: both catenation and the tetravalency of carbon
questions.explanation: The element carbon has the unique property called catenation (self-linkage) due to which it forms covalent bonds with other carbon atoms. It also forms covalent bonds with atoms of other elements like hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur, phosphorus and halogens. It has a valency four because of which it can form four bonds. Because of the above two properties, it can exist in straight chain, branched chain or cyclic forms. It can form single, double a well as triple bonds and hence it can form a large number and variety of compounds.

questions.question: Electronic configuration of an element is:
questions.question_html: Electronic configuration of an element is:

options.text: The distribution of electrons into magnetic states of an atom.
options.html: The distribution of electrons into magnetic states of an atom.

options.text: The distribution of electrons into a spin state of an atom.
options.html: The distribution of electrons into a spin state of an atom.

options.text: The distribution of electrons into different atomic orbitals.
options.html: The distribution of electrons into different atomic orbitals.

options.text: The distribution of electrons into azimuthal orbitals of an atom only.
options.html: The distribution of electrons into azimuthal orbitals of an atom only.

correct_options: The distribution of electrons into different atomic orbitals.
questions.explanation: The electronic configuration of an atom describes the arrangement of electrons in space around the nucleus. The electrons are distributed over different energy levels. These energy levels are called the shell or orbits.

questions.question: Arrange the electrons present in the 4s, 3d, 4p, and 3p orbitals in the order of their increasing energies.
questions.question_html: Arrange the electrons present in the 4s, 3d, 4p, and 3p orbitals in the order of their increasing energies.
questions.explanation: Energy of the orbital is determined by the n+l value. The orbital having a higher n+l value will have higher energy. If n+l value is the same then orbital having a higher n value will have higher energy.

questions.question:
Straight or branched chain compounds, for example, the below ones are called

 ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - CH - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits^{\text{|}} }\limits^{{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{{\text{||}}} }\limits^{\text{O}} {\text{ - H}}$
questions.question_html:
Straight or branched chain compounds, for example, the below ones are called
{tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - CH - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits^{\text{|}} }\limits^{{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{{\text{||}}} }\limits^{\text{O}} {\text{ - H}}{/tex}
questions.explanation: The straight and branched chain compounds are known as aliphatic compounds (also called acyclic or open chain compounds).

questions.question:
In BF_(3), molecule below the dipole moment is zero although the B - F bonds are oriented at an angle of 120° to one another. Net dipole moment in BF_(3), molecule is

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/imgur/EpER5nT.png)
questions.question_html: In BF3, molecule below the dipole moment is zero although the B - F bonds are oriented at an angle of 120° to one another. Net dipole moment in BF3, molecule is
questions.explanation: BF_(3) has symmetrical structure in which the three B-F bonds are oriented at an angle of 120° to one another. Also the three bonds lie in one plane and the dipole moments of these bonds cancel one another giving net dipole moment equal to zero.

questions.question: The oxidation number of hydrogen in LiH, NaH and CaH_(7 )is_____.
questions.question_html: The oxidation number of hydrogen in LiH, NaH and CaH7 is_____.
questions.explanation:
This is according to the rules of assigning oxidation number i.e.

Metal hydrides, such as NaH, LiH, etc., in which the oxidation state for H is -1.

questions.question: Calgon is used in:
questions.question_html: Calgon is used in:
questions.explanation: Calgon is a process of removing the permanent hardness of the water. Calgon is a complex salt, Sodium hexametaphosphate Na(PO_(3))_(6), or Na_(6)P_(6)O_(18). It acts as an ion exchange resin. It is used for the softening of water. When hard water containing Ca and Mg ions are reacted with Calgon, it displaces these ions with Sodium-ion, by forming a complex with itself and thus the water becomes free of these ions which cause hardness.

questions.question: Change in hybridisation affects the carbon’s and the organic compound’s:
questions.question_html: Change in hybridisation affects the carbon’s and the organic compound’s:
questions.explanation:
In hybridization as % of 's' character increases electronegativity also increases.

More the s character more will be the electronegativity of that atom.

Increasing order of electronegativity with %s character is sp (50%) > sp^(2)(33.33%) > sp^(3)(25%).

questions.question: Atomic number of element Unnilhexium is:
questions.question_html: Atomic number of element Unnilhexium is:
questions.explanation: un = 1 Nil = 0 Hex = 6. So, 106 = uninilhexium.

questions.question: Consider the ground state of Cr atom (Z = 24). The numbers of electrons with the azimuthal quantum numbers, l = 1 and 2 are, respectively:
questions.question_html: Consider the ground state of Cr atom (Z = 24). The numbers of electrons with the azimuthal quantum numbers, l = 1 and 2 are, respectively:
questions.explanation:
Azimuthal quantum number l = 1 is for p and l = 2 is for d.

Now Cr has configuration $1s^2, 2s^2, 2p^6, 3s^2, 3p^6, 3d^5, 4s^1 $ 

Hence there are 12, p-electrons and 5, d-electrons

questions.question:
$6{I^ - }(aq) + 2MnO_4^ - (aq) + 4{H_2}O(I) $ $\longrightarrow$ $3{I_2}(s) + 2MnO(s) + 8O{H^ - }(aq)$ 

The above balanced ionic equation represents a redox reaction where one of the following takes place.
questions.question_html:
{tex}6{I^ - }(aq) + 2MnO_4^ - (aq) + 4{H_2}O(I) {/tex}  {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}3{I_2}(s) + 2MnO(s) + 8O{H^ - }(aq){/tex}
The above balanced ionic equation represents a redox reaction where one of the following takes place.

options.text: iodide ion in basic solution oxidizes permanganate ion
options.html: iodide ion in basic solution oxidizes permanganate ion

options.text: permanganate ion in basic solution oxidizes water
options.html: permanganate ion in basic solution oxidizes water

options.text: permanganate (VII) ion in basic solution reduces iodide ion to produce molecular iodine
options.html: permanganate (VII) ion in basic solution reduces iodide ion to produce molecular iodine

options.text: $MnO_4^ - $ in basic solution oxidizes I^(-) to produce I_(2)
options.html: {tex}MnO_4^ - {/tex} in basic solution oxidizes I- to produce I2

correct_options: $MnO_4^ - $ in basic solution oxidizes I^(-) to produce I_(2)
questions.explanation: Mn in $MnO_4^ - $ (Oxidation state = +7) is itself getting reduced to MnO_(2) (+4) and I^(​​​​​​-) (Oxidation state = -1) is getting oxidized to I_(2 )(Oxidation state = 0).

questions.question: For N_(2), bond order is:
questions.question_html: For N2, bond order is:
questions.explanation: Bond order is the number of chemical bonds between a pair of atoms. in diatomic nitrogen N≡N, Triple covalent bond is present in nitrogen so the bond order is 3.

questions.question: The following reaction: $C{H_3}C{H_2}I + KOH(aq) \to C{H_3}C{H_2}OH + KI$ is classified as:
questions.question_html: The following reaction: {tex}C{H_3}C{H_2}I + KOH(aq) \to C{H_3}C{H_2}OH + KI{/tex} is classified as:
questions.explanation: In this reaction we can observe that I- is replaced or substituted by a negatively charged ion -OH which is a nucleophile. So it is a nucleophilic substitution reaction.

questions.question: The mass of an atom of nitrogen in amu is,
questions.question_html: The mass of an atom of nitrogen in amu is,
questions.explanation:
The atomic mass of Nitrogen ( ^( )_(7)N^(14) ) is 14.

 $\therefore$ 1 atomic mass unit (amu) of N would contain 6.023 $\times$ 10^(23 ) atoms

or, 6.023 $\times$ 10^(23 )atoms of nitrogen (N) would weight= 14 amu

& atom of nitrogen (N) would weight 

= [ $\frac{14}{{6.023} \times {10^{23}}}$ ] amu

questions.question: One of the following options is not used for explaining atomic radius:
questions.question_html: One of the following options is not used for explaining atomic radius:
questions.explanation: The distance from the centre of the nucleus to the outermost shell containing the electrons is called the atomic radius. There are four types that are widely used: covalent radii, metallic radii, van der Waal's radii, and ionic radii.

questions.question: In the reaction of metallic cobalt placed in nickel sulphate solution, there is a competition for release of electrons At equilibrium, chemical tests reveal that both Ni^(2+) (aq) and Co^(2+) (aq) are present at moderate concentrations. The result is that:
questions.question_html: In the reaction of metallic cobalt placed in nickel sulphate solution, there is a competition for release of electrons At equilibrium, chemical tests reveal that both Ni2+ (aq) and Co2+ (aq) are present at moderate concentrations. The result is that:

options.html: Only {tex}{\text{Co}}_2^ + {/tex} (aq) and Ni (s)] are favoured

options.text: Only one reactant and one product is greatly favoured.
options.html: Only one reactant and one product is greatly favoured.

options.text: Neither the reactants nor the products are greatly favoured
options.html: Neither the reactants nor the products are greatly favoured

correct_options: Neither the reactants nor the products are greatly favoured
questions.explanation: Reaction will not complete and it will remain at equilibrium.

questions.question: The axial overlap between the two orbitals leads to the formation of a
questions.question_html: The axial overlap between the two orbitals leads to the formation of a
questions.explanation:
Sigma bond: This type of covalent bond is formed by the axial or end to end overlapping of half-filled atomic orbitals of the atoms participating in bonding. The electron cloud formed as a result of axial overlap is cylindrically symmetrical about an internuclear axis.

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/cbse/review/ques_68429.png)

questions.question:
The IUPAC name of the following compound is

 $ {\text{H - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{\text{|}}^{\text{|}} }\limits_{\text{H}}^{\text{H}} {\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{\text{|}}^{\text{|}} }\limits_{\text{H}}^{\text{H}} {\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{\text{|}}^{\text{|}} }\limits_{\text{H}}^{{\text{Cl}}} {\text{ - Cl}}$
questions.question_html:
The IUPAC name of the following compound is
{tex} {\text{H - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{\text{|}}^{\text{|}} }\limits_{\text{H}}^{\text{H}} {\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{\text{|}}^{\text{|}} }\limits_{\text{H}}^{\text{H}} {\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{\text{|}}^{\text{|}} }\limits_{\text{H}}^{{\text{Cl}}} {\text{ - Cl}}{/tex}
questions.explanation: The given compound has 3 C atoms in the longest chain and 2 chloro groups are attached at position 1. So its IUPAC name is 1, 1-dichloropropane.

questions.question: In scientific notation for numbers, any number can be represented in the form, N $\times$ 10^(n )where,
questions.question_html: In scientific notation for numbers, any number can be represented in the form, N {tex}\times{/tex} 10n where,

options.text: n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary from 0 to 1.
options.html: n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary from 0 to 1.

options.text: n is an exponent having positive values only and N can vary from 1 to 10.
options.html: n is an exponent having positive values only and N can vary from 1 to 10.

options.text: n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary from 10 to 100.
options.html: n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary from 10 to 100.

options.text: n is an exponent having positive or negative values. N represents a number that can vary from 1 to 10, but not 10 itself.
options.html: n is an exponent having positive or negative values. N represents a number that can vary from 1 to 10, but not 10 itself.

correct_options: n is an exponent having positive or negative values. N represents a number that can vary from 1 to 10, but not 10 itself.
questions.explanation: Scientific notation is conveniently used to represent extremely large or even very small numerical values. It is an exponential notation in which any number can be represented in the form, N $\times$ 10^(n), where n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary between 1 and 10, but not 10 itself.

questions.question: Dalton's atomic theory could not explain one of the following
questions.question_html: Dalton's atomic theory could not explain one of the following
questions.explanation:
Dalton’s atomic theory suffered from the following drawbacks:

 Atoms of the same or different types have a strong tendency to combine together to form a new ‘group of atoms’. For example, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen gases exist in nature as ‘group of two atoms’. This indicates that the smallest unit capable of independent existence is not an atom, but a ‘group of atoms’.
 With the discovery of sub-atomic particles, e.g., electrons, neutrons, and protons, the atom can no longer be considered indivisible.
 Discovery of isotopes indicated that all atoms of the same element are not perfectly identical. At least, they differ in their masses. Atoms of the same element having different masses are called isotopes.

questions.question: In which of the following substances will hydrogen bond be strongest?
questions.question_html: In which of the following substances will hydrogen bond be strongest?
questions.explanation: Out of all H_(2)O will form strongest hydrogen bonding due to high electronegativity of O.

questions.question: Heavy hydrogen is also called _______.
questions.question_html: Heavy hydrogen is also called _______.
questions.explanation: Deuterium, (D or ^(2)H), also called heavy hydrogen, an isotope of hydrogen with a nucleus consisting of one proton and one neutron, which is double the mass of the nucleus of ordinary hydrogen (one proton). Deuterium has an atomic weight of 2.014.

questions.question:
What is the correct order of decreasing stability of the following cations?

 $\underset{\text{}}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-C{{H}_{3}}}}\,$ 
 $\underset{}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-OC{{H}_{3}}}}\,$ 
 $\underset{}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-C{{H}_{2}}-OC{{H}_{3}}}}\,$
questions.question_html:
What is the correct order of decreasing stability of the following cations?

  1. {tex}\underset{\text{}}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-C{{H}_{3}}}}\,{/tex}
  2. {tex}\underset{}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-OC{{H}_{3}}}}\,{/tex}
  3. {tex}\underset{}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}-\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{CH}}\,-C{{H}_{2}}-OC{{H}_{3}}}}\,{/tex}
questions.explanation:
In (I) case, + vely charged C is attached to two alkyl groups and +I effect stabilizes carbocation.
 In (II) case, stable due to +R effect of -OCH_(3) group stabilizes carbocation.
 In (III) case, -I effect of -OCH_(3) group. destabilizes carbocation. hence, the order of stability: II > I > III.

questions.question: During estimation of nitrogen present in an organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method, the ammonia evolved from 0.5 g of the compound, neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H_(2)SO_(4.) Find out the percentage of nitrogen in the compound.
questions.question_html: During estimation of nitrogen present in an organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method, the ammonia evolved from 0.5 g of the compound, neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H2SO4. Find out the percentage of nitrogen in the compound.
questions.explanation:
1 M of 10 mL H_(2)SO_(4)= 1 M of 20 mL NH_(3)

1000 mL of 1 M ammonia contains 14 g nitrogen

20 mL of 1 M ammonia contains $\frac{{(14 \times 20g)}}{{1000}}$ nitrogen

% of nitrogen = $ \frac{{(14 \times 20 \times 100)}}{{1000 \times 0.5}}$ = 56.0%

questions.question: The Mass Number of an atom is
questions.question_html: The Mass Number of an atom is

options.text: the total number of nucleons and neutrons
options.html: the total number of nucleons and neutrons

options.text: the total number of electrons and positrons
the total number of protons and electrons
the total number of protons and neutrons
options.html: the total number of protons and neutrons

correct_options: the total number of protons and neutrons
questions.explanation:
The mass number (symbol A), also called atomic mass number or nucleon number, is the total number of protons and neutrons (together known as nucleons) in an atomic nucleus

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/cbse/rvw/ques_21267.png)

questions.question: Species having same bond order are:
questions.question_html: Species having same bond order are:
questions.explanation: bond order of F_(2)^(+) and O_(2)^(-) is same i.e 1.5

questions.question: One of the following is a non-stoichiometric hydride?
questions.question_html: One of the following is a non-stoichiometric hydride?
questions.explanation: Metallic hydrides are usually nonstoichiometric compounds, as expected from their relatively low heats of formation and the mobility of hydrogen. They are ordinarily described, chemically, in terms of any of three models in which hydrogen is considered a small interstitial atom, a proton, or a hydride anion.

questions.question: 2-bromo butane can be represented in various ways. Choose the incorrect one from the following
questions.question_html: 2-bromo butane can be represented in various ways. Choose the incorrect one from the following
questions.explanation: methyl group is represented at 3rd carbon no bromine is there on 2nd carbon

questions.question: The number of moles of solute present in 1 kg of solvent is called:
questions.question_html: The number of moles of solute present in 1 kg of solvent is called:
questions.explanation: Mathematically, molality is expressed as, $Molality (m) ={ No. \ of \ moles \ of \ solute \over Mass\ of\ solvent \ in \ kg }$

questions.question: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 $\times$ 10^(15) Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 $\times$ 10^(-34)Js).
questions.question_html: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34Js).
questions.explanation:
We know Planck's equation is $E= h\nu$ 

where E is energy, h is Planck’s constant and $\nu $ is frequency.

Put the given values,

E = $6.626\times 10^{-34}\times 3\times 10^{15}$ $= 1.988\times 10^{-18}J$

questions.question:
The following compounds are called:

questions.question_html: The following compounds are called:
questions.explanation: Aromatic compounds may also have heteroatom in the ring. Such compounds are called heterocyclic aromatic compounds. Thus the given compounds are aromatic heterocyclic compounds namely furan, thiophene and pyridine respectively.

questions.question: Which of the following has maximum number of lone pairs associated with Xe?
questions.question_html: Which of the following has maximum number of lone pairs associated with Xe?
questions.explanation:
In XeF_(​​​​2): Xe has 2 of its electrons in bonding with F. So, since Xe has 8 valence electrons, the no.of lone pairs = ${no. \ of \ non-bonding \ electrons\over 2}={6\over 2}=3$ 
 In XeO_(3): Xe has 6 of its electrons in bonding with O (bcoz of double bond). So, Xe will have 2 non bonding electrons and therefore 1 lone pair.
 In XeF_(4): Xe has 4 of its electrons in bonding with F. So, Xe will have 4 non bonding electrons and therefore 2 lone pairs.
 In XeF_(6): Xe has 6 of its electrons in bonding with F. So, Xe will have 2 non bonding electrons and therefore 1 lone pair.

So XeF_(2) has the maximum no. of lone pairs associated with Xe.

questions.question: In the naming of organic compounds containing polyfunctional compounds, one of the functional groups is chosen as the principal functional group and the compound is then named on that basis. The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:
questions.question_html: In the naming of organic compounds containing polyfunctional compounds, one of the functional groups is chosen as the principal functional group and the compound is then named on that basis. The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:

options.text: - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_(2), > C = C <, - C $\equiv$ C -, -CONH_(2)
options.html: - CN, - HC = O, > C = O,  - OH, - NH2, > C = C <, - C {tex}\equiv{/tex} C -, -CONH2

options.text: - HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_(2), > C = C <, - C $\equiv $ C -, -CONH_(2), - CN
options.html: - HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH2, > C = C <, - C {tex}\equiv {/tex}C -, -CONH2, - CN

options.text: - C = O, - OH, - NH_(2), > C = C <, - C $\equiv$ C -, -CONH_(2), - CN > - HC = O
options.html: - C = O, - OH, - NH2, > C = C <, - C {tex}\equiv{/tex} C -, -CONH2, - CN > - HC = O

options.text: -CONH_(2),-CN, -HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_(2),> C = C <, -C $\equiv$ C-

correct_options: -CONH_(2),-CN, -HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_(2),> C = C <, -C $\equiv$ C-
questions.explanation:
The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:

-CONH_(2), -CN, -HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_(2), > C = C <, -C $\equiv$ C-

Carboxlic acid > sulphonic acid > acid anhydride > ester > acyl halide > acyl amide > nitrile > aldehyde > ketones > alcohol > amine > alkene > alkyne.

questions.question: The pair of ions having same electronic configuration is ________.
questions.question_html: The pair of ions having same electronic configuration is ________.
questions.explanation:
Explanation: Fe(Z-26: 3d^(6)4s^(2)) and Mn (Z-25: 3d^(5)4s^(2))

Fe^(3+): 3d^(5) and Mn^(2+): 3d^(5) will have the same no. of electrons and hence, the same electronic configuration.

questions.question: Which of the following is a property of a carbocation?
questions.question_html: Which of the following is a property of a carbocation?

options.text: it has a sextet of electrons
options.html: it has a sextet of electrons

questions.explanation: A species having a carbon atom possessing a sextet of electrons and a positive charge is called a carbocation (earlier called carbonium ion). Carbocations are highly unstable and reactive species. They are formed by heterolytic cleavage.

questions.question: The atom with the given atomic number Z=17, and the atomic mass A=35 is:
questions.question_html: The atom with the given atomic number Z=17, and the atomic mass A=35 is:
questions.explanation: The atomic number of Chlorine is 17 which is a common nonmetallic element belonging to the halogens; best known as a heavy yellow irritating toxic gas; used to purify water and as a bleaching agent and disinfectant; occurs naturally only as a salt (as in seawater).

questions.question:
Assertion (A): It is impossible to determine the exact position and exact momentum of an electron simultaneously.

Reason (R): The path of an electron in an atom is clearly defined.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): It is impossible to determine the exact position and exact momentum of an electron simultaneously.
Reason (R): The path of an electron in an atom is clearly defined.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is false but R is true
options.html: A is false but R is true

correct_options: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation: The effect of the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle is significant only for the motion of microscopic objects and is negligible for that of macroscopic objects.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Among the Mg, Mg^(2+), Al, Al^(3+) the smallest radius is of Al^(3+).

Reason (R): Cations are larger than their parent atoms.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): Among the Mg, Mg2+, Al, Al3+ the smallest radius is of Al3+.
Reason (R): Cations are larger than their parent atoms.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

correct_options: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation: Atomic radii decrease across a period. Cations are smaller than their parent atoms. Among isoelectronic species, the one with the larger positive nuclear charge will have a smaller radius. Hence the largest species is Mg; the smallest one is Al^(3+).

questions.question:
Assertion (A): CHCI_(3) and CH_(3)OH are miscible.

Reason (R): One of them is polar.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): CHCI3 and CH3OH are miscible.
Reason (R): One of them is polar.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_options: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation: $C H C l_{3}$ and $C H_{3}-O H$ are miscible due to intermolecular van der Waals force of attraction. However, $C H_{3} O H$ and $C H C l_{3}$ are polar molecules.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Oxidation number of Ni in [Ni(CO)_(4)] is zero.

Reason (R): Nickel is bonded to neutral ligand, carbonyl.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): Oxidation number of Ni in [Ni(CO)4] is zero.
Reason (R): Nickel is bonded to neutral ligand, carbonyl.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation:
O. No. of Ni + 4 (O. No. of CO) = 0

[as O. No. of CO = 0 ( $\therefore$ neutral ligand)]

 $\therefore$ O. No. of Ni = 0

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Isomorphous substances form crystals of the same shape and can grow in a saturated solution of each other.

Reason (R): They have a similar constitution and chemical formulae.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): Isomorphous substances form crystals of the same shape and can grow in a saturated solution of each other.
Reason (R): They have a similar constitution and chemical formulae.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation: Examples of isomorphous compounds are K_(2)SO_(4), K_(2)CrO_(4), K_(2)SeO_(4), (valency of S, Cr, Se = 6) and ZnSO_(4) $\cdot$ 7H_(2)O, MgSO_(4) $\cdot$ 7H_(2)O FeSO_(4) $\cdot$ 7H_(2)O (valency of Zn, Mg, Fe = 2).

questions.question:
Consider the reaction,

 $2{C_4}{H_{10}}(g) + 13{O_2}(g) \to 8C{O_2}(g) + 10{H_2}O(l)$ 

If 300g of C_(4)H_(10) (g) is burnt with 1000 g of O_(2), the yield of H_(2)O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are _____ and _____ respectively.
questions.question_html:
Consider the  reaction,
{tex}2{C_4}{H_{10}}(g) + 13{O_2}(g) \to 8C{O_2}(g) + 10{H_2}O(l){/tex}
If 300g of C4H10 (g) is burnt with 1000 g of O2, the yield of H2O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are _____ and _____ respectively.
questions.explanation:
Calculations

As per the stoichiometry of the given equation,

 $2{C_4}{H_{10}}(g) + 13{O_2}(g) \to 8C{O_2}(g) + 10{H_2}O(l)$ 

Calculations:

Step 1/ -

Molar mass of C_(4)H_(10)

= (4 $\times$ 2 + 1 $\times$ 10) amu = (48 + 10) amu

= 58 amu

 $\therefore$ 300g of C_(4)H_(10)

= $\frac{300}{58}$ moles

= 5.17 moles

Step 2 / -

 For the yield of H_(2)O;

 Since 2 mols of C_(4)H_(10) yields 10 H_(2)O i.e.

 = 10 moles.

 $\therefore$ 5.17 moles of C_(4)H_(10) would yield 10H_(2)O_( )

 $ = \frac{{5.17 \times 10}}{2}moles$ 

 = 25.85 moles

 $ = (25.85 \times 18)g$ 

 465.3 g $\approx$ 465 g
 For predicting the limiting reagent;
 
 Calculate the number of moles of O_(2) needed to react with 5.17 moles of C_(4)H_(10)

 Since 2 moles of C_(4)H_(10) requires 13 O_(2) molecules i.e.

 = 13 moles

 $ = \frac{{13 \times 5.17}}{2}moles$ 

 = 33.62 moles

 Number of moles of O_(2 )actually taken for combustion

 = $\frac{mass \ of \ oxygen \ taken }{ molar \ mass \ of \ oxygen}$ 

 = $\frac{1000}{32} moles$ 

 = 31.25 moles

 Thus, it is noteworthy that,

 O_(2(g)) taken for reaction (i.e. 31.25 moles) is lesser than the required number of moles (i.e. 33.62 moles).

 Hence, it would be consumed first and is, therefore, the limiting reagent for this reaction.

questions.question: How many neutrons and protons are there in the $^{13}_6C$ nucleus?
questions.question_html: How many neutrons and protons are there in the  {tex}^{13}_6C{/tex} nucleus?
questions.explanation:
Number of protons = Atomic number =6

Number of neutrons = atomic mass - no of protons = 13-6 = 7

questions.question: The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups in the naming of an organic compound is:
questions.question_html: The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups in the naming of an organic compound is:

options.text: - CONH_(2), - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_(2), - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl
options.html: - CONH<sub>2</sub>, - CN, - HC = O,  > C = O,  - OH,  - NH<sub>2</sub>,  - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl

options.text: - COOR (R = alkyl group right), - COCl, - CONH_(2 ), - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - H_(2)
options.html: - COOR (R = alkyl group right), - COCl, - CONH<sub>2 </sub>, - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - H<sub>2</sub>

options.text: - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_(2), - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl, - CONH_(2)
options.html: - CN, - HC = O,  > C = O, - OH, - NH<sub>2</sub>, - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl, - CONH<sub>2</sub>

options.text: - COCl, - CONH_(2), - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_(2), - COOR (R = alkyl group)
options.html: - COCl, - CONH<sub>2</sub>, - CN, - HC = O,  > C = O,  - OH,  - NH<sub>2</sub>, - COOR (R = alkyl group)

correct_options: - COOR (R = alkyl group right), - COCl, - CONH_(2 ), - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - H_(2)
questions.explanation:
COOR (R=alkyl group) > -COCl,> -CONH_(2 )> -CN > -HC=O> >C=O> -OH, -NH_(2) is the decreasing order of priority

ester > acyl halide > acyl amide > nitrile > aldehyde > ketones > alcohol > amine

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
Every experimental measurement has some amount of uncertainty associated with it because of the limitation of the measuring instrument and the skill of the person making the measurement. Significant figures are meaningful digits that are known with certainty plus one which is estimated or uncertain. The uncertainty is Density involved mass and volume. The density of a substance is its amount of mass per unit volume. The density of a substance tells us about how closely its particles are packed. If the density is more, it means particles are more closely packed. Temperature are three common scales to measure temperature — °C (degree celsius), Fahrenheit) and K (kelvin). Here, K is the SI unit. The thermometers are based on these scales indicate by writing the certain digits and the last uncertain digit. Precision refers to the closeness of various measurements for the same quantity. However, accuracy is the agreement of a particular value to the true value of the result. 

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/imgur/1632228481-zgyzfb.jpg) 

The significant figures in 3400 are
